stories.headline: Denver Refuses DOJ Demand to Repeal 37-Year-Old Assault Weapons Ban
stories.summary:
Denver Mayor Mike Johnston on Monday publicly rejected a Trump administration ultimatum to repeal the city's 37-year-old ban on assault-style weapons and large-capacity magazines, vowing to defend the ordinance in court if necessary. The U.S. Department of Justice had demanded Denver and the state of Colorado end the bans or face a federal lawsuit. Denver Police Chief Ron Thomas joined city officials and anti-gun-violence advocates on the steps of the City and County Building to announce the defiant stance.

The city's ban on assault-style weapons has been in place for nearly four decades. State officials similarly rejected the DOJ's demands regarding Colorado's own restrictions on large-capacity magazines. Both the city and state indicated they view the federal threat as legally challengeable and politically motivated.

The confrontation marks a significant escalation in tensions between Denver and the Trump administration over local gun regulations. City attorneys are expected to prepare legal defenses should the DOJ follow through on its lawsuit threat. The standoff draws national attention to the question of whether federal authority can override long-standing local firearms ordinances.

stories.headline: RTD Board Overhaul Advances as Colorado House Passes Bill to Shrink Transit Agency Governance
stories.summary:
The Colorado House passed Senate Bill 150 on Monday on a 39-26 vote, setting up a major restructuring of the Regional Transportation District's governing board. The bill would reduce the all-elected, 15-member board to nine members in a hybrid structure: five elected representatives and four appointed by the governor. The measure now needs a final procedural Senate vote before heading to Gov. Jared Polis, who is expected to sign it.

Under the new structure, one gubernatorial appointee would come from a nominee list prepared by the Denver Regional Council of Governments, and another must be a current or former member of the Amalgamated Transit Union. Board member pay would also increase from $12,000 to $36,000 annually, with the elected chair earning more. Changes would take effect beginning with the 2028 election cycle.

The bill emerged from the RTD Accountability Committee, which was created by lawmakers in response to ongoing concerns about the agency's post-pandemic performance. Critics of the hybrid board model, including retired public policy consultant Miller Hudson, warned that appointed members would reduce the agency's responsiveness to residents across its eight-county service area. Supporters argue the overhaul is necessary to deliver the reliable transit service needed to support dense housing development along the Front Range.

stories.headline: Polis Administration Backs Investment Bill for Child Care Using 'Super-Secret' Legal Memo
stories.summary:
Gov. Jared Polis is pursuing legislation that would allow state agencies to invest enterprise fees and public money in privately managed investment funds to generate revenue for Colorado's struggling child care assistance program. The governor's office is relying on what one lawmaker called a 'super-secret memo' from the attorney general's office for legal backing — a memo that appears to contradict a separate, public attorney general opinion stating the Colorado Constitution generally prohibits the state from directly owning corporate stock.

Senate Bill 180, sponsored by Loveland Democrat Sen. Janice Marchman, would create an 'Investment Performance Authority,' an unpaid board overseen by gubernatorial appointees and the state budget director, to manage the private investments. Proceeds would fund the Colorado Child Care Assistance Program, which has faced dire enrollment freezes and waitlists in recent years as counties struggle to meet demand.

State Treasurer Dave Young, whose office requested the public attorney general opinion and opposes the bill, warned that the measure invites unacceptable risk to the public treasury. He said it was the first bill his office had outright opposed in his more than seven years in the position. SB-180 passed a narrow committee vote and continues to advance through the legislature despite the dueling legal interpretations.

stories.headline: Colorado Legislature Passes $48.6 Billion State Budget With Medicaid Cuts
stories.summary:
The Colorado legislature has passed a $48.6 billion state budget and sent it to Gov. Jared Polis for his signature. The spending plan includes cuts to Medicaid, reflecting difficult fiscal choices lawmakers faced amid constraints on state revenue and growing demand for health services. The budget represents one of the most consequential pieces of legislation from the current legislative session.

Details of the Medicaid reductions and other line-item decisions are expected to draw scrutiny from health care advocates and providers across the state. Lawmakers balanced competing priorities including transportation, education, and social services within the budget framework. The passage of the budget comes as the legislature wraps up a busy final stretch of its session.

The budget's Medicaid component is particularly significant for Denver metro residents, who rely heavily on the state-federal health insurance program. Polis has not yet indicated whether he will sign the budget as passed or seek any amendments.

stories.headline: Denver City Council Unanimously Extends Site Development Plan Deadlines to Rescue 22,600 Housing Units
stories.summary:
The Denver City Council voted unanimously Monday to extend the expiration dates of previously approved site development plans by 36 months, covering all plans approved on or before Dec. 31, 2025. City officials said the move was necessary to preserve approximately 22,600 planned residential units at risk of lapsing due to a sharp decline in construction financing activity. The extension amends Denver's zoning code and came in response to what city planners described as unique economic conditions.

Elevated construction costs, high interest rates, declining rents, and constrained capital markets have combined to stall projects that cleared city approval hurdles during the construction boom of 2021 and 2022. Brad Buchanan, executive director of Denver's community planning and development, told council members the city faces a rare opportunity to capture approved projects before rents spike again in two to three years.

Developers including one owner of a parking lot at 17th Street and California Street in District 10 — where a 38-story, 655,000-square-foot mixed-use tower was approved last April — cited the extension as critical to assembling financing. The city has set a 2026 goal of delivering 2,500 affordable housing units and permitting an additional 5,000. Council members framed the extension as a common-sense bridge to preserve the development pipeline during an extraordinarily difficult market.

stories.headline: High-End Co-Working Brand Industrious to Open Fourth Denver Location in Former WeWork Space in LoHi
stories.summary:
Industrious, a premium flexible office company, is moving into a former WeWork space on Platte Street in Denver's LoHi neighborhood, marking the brand's fourth location in the city. The new facility will include 379 office seats, catering to businesses and professionals seeking flexible workspace arrangements in one of Denver's most active commercial corridors.

The expansion reflects continued demand for co-working and flex-office space in Denver even as the broader commercial real estate market grapples with elevated vacancy rates downtown. Industrious has positioned itself in the higher-end segment of the co-working market, differentiating from the WeWork model that previously occupied the space.

The LoHi location adds to Industrious's existing Denver footprint and signals continued private-sector investment in flexible office infrastructure in the metro area. Terms of the lease were not disclosed.

stories.headline: Update: Second Person Dies in Southwest Denver Go-Kart Crash, Both Victims Identified as Go-Kart Occupants
stories.summary:
A second person has died following a collision between a go-kart and a car late Saturday night in southwest Denver, the Denver Police Department confirmed Monday. The crash occurred just after 11:20 p.m. near West Evans Avenue and South Federal Boulevard, on the edge of the Harvey Park and College View neighborhoods. One victim died at the scene; the second died later at a hospital.

Both victims were occupants of the go-kart, a police spokesperson confirmed. Go-karts are generally not legal to operate on public streets in Colorado. The Denver Office of the Medical Examiner is responsible for formally identifying both individuals.

As of Monday afternoon, no arrests had been made and no citations had been issued. The cause of the crash and determination of fault remained under investigation. Police provided no additional information about the driver of the vehicle involved in the collision.

stories.headline: Spring Snowstorm to Deliver 3 to 9 Inches to Denver Metro, Freezing Temps to Follow Through Thursday
stories.summary:
A late-season winter storm is expected to bring 3 to 9 inches of snow to the Denver metro area Tuesday through Wednesday, with the most significant accumulations arriving overnight Tuesday into Wednesday morning. National Weather Service meteorologist Russell Danielson said the Wednesday morning commute is likely to be the most hazardous, as warm road temperatures Tuesday should limit accumulation during the day. Heavy, wet snow could cause tree branch and power line damage, with power outages possible.

A freeze watch has been issued for large portions of eastern and northeastern Colorado — including Boulder, Golden, Greeley, Littleton, Denver International Airport, Fort Lupton and Castle Rock — from Wednesday night through Thursday morning. Temperatures could drop as low as 23 degrees, posing a risk to crops and unprotected outdoor plumbing. Residents were advised to take steps to protect plants before the cold arrives.

In the mountains, snowfall totals could be far more dramatic. The National Weather Service's expected scenario projects 18 to 36 inches along the Continental Divide north of Interstate 70, with roughly 18 to 24 inches near the Eisenhower Tunnel. A lower-probability high-end scenario puts totals near Longs Peak at 48 to 60 inches. Danielson noted the precipitation is welcome amid an extreme drought, but falls well short of the amount needed to reverse the state's water deficit.

stories.headline: Rockies Baseball Ops Chief DePodesta Calls 14-21 Start 'Positive First Month' in Year-One Rebuild
stories.summary:
Colorado Rockies president of baseball operations Paul DePodesta offered an optimistic assessment of the team's first month Sunday, calling the 14-21 start — despite a weekend sweep at the hands of the Atlanta Braves at Coors Field — a meaningful improvement over last season's historically bad 6-29 mark through the same stretch. DePodesta, hired last November following the franchise's 119-loss season, said pitching has been the most consistent element of the early season.

The three veteran free-agent starters signed in the offseason — right-handers Tomoyuki Sugano and Michael Lorenzen and lefthander Jose Quintana, acquired for a combined $18.85 million — have posted a collective 4.81 ERA, dropping to 3.97 over the last 15 games. DePodesta also addressed the team's use of an 'opener' strategy ahead of Chase Dollander, explaining it is designed to maximize Dollander's innings rather than to avoid specific opposing hitters. Dollander is 3-2 with a 3.38 ERA through eight appearances.

DePodesta expressed patience with struggling outfielders Jordan Beck (.167 average, .469 OPS) and Brenton Doyle (.213 average), warning against reactionary roster moves. The Rockies entered Monday hosting the New York Mets after the Braves series.

stories.headline: Justin Simmons Formally Retires as a Bronco, Ending Career a Decade After Being Drafted by Denver
stories.summary:
Justin Simmons officially retired as a member of the Denver Broncos on Monday, closing out a professional career that began when Denver selected the safety in the third round of the 2016 NFL Draft. Simmons spent the bulk of his career with the Broncos, becoming one of the team's most respected defensive players and earning multiple Pro Bowl selections before departing in free agency ahead of the 2024 season.

Simmons returned to Denver in a ceremonial capacity to sign retirement papers and conclude his career with the franchise where he made his name. The safety was known for his durability, coverage ability, and leadership in the Broncos' locker room over nearly a decade. He was released by Denver after the 2023 season in a cost-cutting move before playing his final NFL season with the Atlanta Falcons.

The retirement marks a sentimental close to an era for the Denver defense. Simmons's career overlapped with some of the franchise's most turbulent years following its Super Bowl 50 championship, and he remained a consistent presence through multiple coaching and roster overhauls.

stories.headline: Nuggets' Playoff Exit Prompts Calls for Sweeping Roster and Coaching Staff Changes
stories.summary:
Denver Nuggets fans and analysts are calling for significant changes to the franchise following the team's first-round playoff exit, with scrutiny falling on both the roster construction and coaching staff. The early elimination has intensified questions about the team's ability to compete for a championship around two-time MVP Nikola Jokic, whose contract extension situation looms as a central offseason decision.

The debate centers on whether head coach Michael Malone's system and the surrounding cast are capable of maximizing Jokic's remaining prime years. Roster questions include the futures of several veterans and role players who underperformed during the postseason series. Front office decisions on trades, free agency signings, and draft strategy will define how the organization responds to the disappointing finish.

The Nuggets won the 2023 NBA championship but have failed to return to the Finals in subsequent seasons. The offseason figures to be one of the most consequential in recent franchise history, with ownership and management facing pressure to make bold moves rather than stand pat.

stories.headline: Federal Charges Filed Against Juvenile in Swatting Incident That Triggered CU Boulder Campus Lockdown
stories.summary:
Federal prosecutors have filed charges against a juvenile in connection with a swatting incident that prompted a campus-wide lockdown at the University of Colorado Boulder. The false emergency report triggered a significant law enforcement response on the CU campus, disrupting students, faculty, and staff. Swatting — the practice of making fraudulent emergency calls to dispatch armed police to a location — has become an increasingly serious federal crime.

The case marks a notable use of federal jurisdiction in a swatting prosecution involving a minor. Details about the specific charges filed and the nature of the false report were not fully disclosed in initial reports. Federal authorities have increasingly pursued swatting cases at the federal level due to their potential for causing harm and their often interstate or online nature.

The CU Boulder incident drew attention to campus safety vulnerabilities and the real-world consequences of swatting hoaxes. University officials and law enforcement have not released additional details about the timeline of the investigation or when the juvenile was identified as a suspect.

weather.notable: About 22 degrees below the average high for this time of year.

joke.joke: I told my kids we're having a fire drill. They said, 'What do we do?' I said, 'Get out of the house.' They said, 'We live here.' I said, 'Not during the drill you don't.'

topStory.headline: Iran Fires Missiles, Drones at U.S. and Allied Ships in Strait of Hormuz as Ceasefire Frays
topStory.summary:
Iran launched cruise missiles, drones and small fast boats against U.S.-flagged commercial vessels and U.S. Navy ships escorting them through the Strait of Hormuz, according to Adm. Brad Cooper, commander of U.S. Central Command. No American vessels were struck, Cooper said, and U.S. attack helicopters destroyed the small boats and intercepted the drones. Both Iran and the United States downplayed the extent of damage to their respective forces, with Iran claiming it had struck an American warship — an assertion Central Command denied — and Iran denying the U.S. had sunk any of its boats.

The clashes occurred as the U.S. Navy carried out a new mission ordered by President Donald Trump to escort commercial ships through the strategically vital waterway. Cooper described U.S. forces as acting in a purely defensive capacity, providing what he called a "thick layer of defense" to allow stranded vessels to transit out of the Arabian Gulf. When asked directly whether the U.S.-Iran ceasefire that took effect April 8 had collapsed, Cooper declined to say, though he acknowledged Iran was "initiating aggressive behavior."

The incidents extended beyond American vessels. The United Arab Emirates said it intercepted 12 ballistic missiles, 3 cruise missiles and 4 drones fired from Iran over the weekend, resulting in three minor injuries — the first Iranian strikes on the UAE since the ceasefire was declared roughly a month ago. A South Korean-operated, Panama-flagged cargo ship also suffered an explosion and fire while anchored near the UAE in the strait, which Seoul's foreign ministry described as a "possible attack." All 24 crew members, including six South Koreans, were reported unharmed.

Trump addressed the events Monday on Truth Social, acknowledging that U.S. forces had shot down seven Iranian fast boats and that Iran had "taken some shots" at other countries, while encouraging South Korea to join the American-led escort mission. He did not acknowledge in a separate post that any Iranian fire had been directed at U.S.-flagged vessels. The Strait of Hormuz is one of the world's most critical oil shipping chokepoints, and sustained military activity there carries significant implications for global energy markets and regional stability.

The episode represents the most serious test yet of the April ceasefire between Washington and Tehran, which was reached following a brief but intense period of direct U.S. military strikes on Iranian nuclear and military facilities. Whether the current hostilities constitute a formal breakdown of that agreement remains unresolved, with both governments so far avoiding language that would explicitly declare the ceasefire void. Analysts and allied governments will be watching closely in the coming days for further Iranian action and any escalatory response from Washington.
